---
title: "Soil Health Impacts on Water Storage & Hydraulic Conductivity"
toc: false
---

How do **soil texture**, **bulk density** (compaction), and **organic matter** control how
much water a soil can store and transmit? Increasing soil organic
matter and decompacting disturbed soils are two proven outcomes in soil restoration and both benefit soil hydrology.

These pages turn the USDA [**ROSETTA3**](https://github.com/usda-ars-ussl/rosetta-soil)
pedotransfer functions into **interactive charts** you can explore in your browser.
They also blend in findings from
[Minasny & McBratney (2018)](https://bsssjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/ejss.12475)
on how much soil organic matter affects water storage. Drag the sliders to change
bulk density or organic matter and watch porosity, plant-available water, and infiltration
respond.

::: {.callout-tip}
## How to explore
Every chart is live: **drag the sliders** (bulk density, organic matter) and **hover** for
exact values; use the toolbar to **zoom and pan**. Grey, dashed regions flag texture and bulk-density values
outside of the models calibration.
:::

## Soil health at a glance: how compaction & organic matter change water storage

Drag the two sliders — **bulk density** (compaction) and **organic matter** — and watch the
soil-water graph respond. Easing compaction and adding organic matter increase both 
**plant-available** and **drainable** water storage capacity. The table below the chart also responds
to the sliders, providing soil water retention values.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "notebooks")  # index.qmd executes at the repo root (execute-dir: file)

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import holoviews as hv
import panel as pn
from _helpers import (
    soil_water_texture_band_diagram,
    soil_water_bd_om_blend_table,
    soil_water_table_html,
)

pn.extension()

result = pd.read_csv("notebooks/rosetta_porosity_by_texture.csv")
TEXTURE_CLASSES = list(result["texture_class"].drop_duplicates())
texture_x = {cls: i for i, cls in enumerate(TEXTURE_CLASSES)}
HSG = dict(result[["texture_class", "hydrologic_soil_group"]].drop_duplicates().itertuples(index=False, name=None))
texture_ticks = [(i, f"{cls} ({HSG[cls]})") for cls, i in texture_x.items()]
INCHES_PER_FOOT = 12.0

bulk_densities = [round(float(b), 1) for b in np.sort(result["bulk_density_g_cm3"].unique())]
om_grid = [round(float(o), 1) for o in np.arange(0.0, 8.0 + 1e-9, 1.0)]

# One shared pair of sliders drives BOTH the figure and the table; placed BETWEEN them.
_SLIDER_CSS = [":host, :host * { font-size: 1rem; }"]  # enlarge slider title + tick labels
bd_slider = pn.widgets.DiscreteSlider(
    name="Bulk density, g/cm³ (higher is more compacted)", options=bulk_densities, value=1.4,
    width=380, stylesheets=_SLIDER_CSS)
om_slider = pn.widgets.DiscreteSlider(
    name="Organic matter (% by weight)", options=om_grid, value=2.0,
    width=380, stylesheets=_SLIDER_CSS)


def _figure(bd, om):
    t = soil_water_bd_om_blend_table(result, bd, om)
    x = np.array([texture_x[c] for c in TEXTURE_CLASSES])
    pwp = t["wilting_point_porosity"].to_numpy() * INCHES_PER_FOOT
    fc = t["field_capacity_porosity"].to_numpy() * INCHES_PER_FOOT
    por = t["total_porosity"].to_numpy() * INCHES_PER_FOOT
    ov = soil_water_texture_band_diagram(
        x, pwp, fc, por,
        texture_labels=t["texture_class"].astype(str).tolist(),
        implausible=t["implausible"].to_numpy(),
    )
    return ov.opts(
        hv.opts.Overlay(width=720, height=520, toolbar="right", legend_position="top_left",
                        xlabel="texture class (hydrologic soil group); coarse → fine",
                        ylabel="Water Storage Capacity (inches per foot of soil depth)",
                        title="Soil water storage vs. texture — ROSETTA + Minasny & McBratney blend"),
        hv.opts.Curve(xticks=texture_ticks, xrotation=45, ylim=(0, 10)),
        hv.opts.Area(xticks=texture_ticks, xrotation=45, ylim=(0, 10)),
    )


def _table(bd, om):
    t = soil_water_bd_om_blend_table(result, bd, om)
    out = pd.DataFrame({
        "texture (HSG)": [f"{c} ({HSG[c]})" for c in t["texture_class"]],
        "wilting point": t["wilting_point_porosity"].to_numpy(),
        "field capacity": t["field_capacity_porosity"].to_numpy(),
        "saturation": t["total_porosity"].to_numpy(),
        "available water": t["available_water_capacity"].to_numpy(),
        "drainable water": t["drainable_water"].to_numpy(),
    }).round(3)
    return pn.pane.HTML(soil_water_table_html(out), width=720)


layout = pn.Column(
    pn.panel(pn.bind(_figure, bd_slider, om_slider)),
    pn.Row(bd_slider, om_slider),
    pn.pane.HTML(
        "<b>Soil water retention by texture class (porosity volume fraction, cm³/cm³)</b>",
        styles={"font-size": "1rem", "margin": "8px 0 2px"},
    ),
    pn.panel(pn.bind(_table, bd_slider, om_slider)),
    width=790,
)

# Bake every (bulk density, organic matter) state into static HTML so the sliders work with no
# kernel (12 BD x 9 OM = 108 states). embed() SILENTLY truncates if its limits are exceeded —
# leaving dead/stale frames in the static page — so assert the bounds (max_opts must exceed the
# options per slider; max_states must exceed their product) to fail loudly if the grids grow.
assert len(bulk_densities) <= 40 and len(om_grid) <= 40, "max_opts=40 too low for slider options"
assert len(bulk_densities) * len(om_grid) <= 2000, "max_states=2000 too low for BD×OM combos"
layout.embed(max_states=2000, max_opts=40, progress=False)
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Figure axis is in **inches per foot of soil depth**; the table lists the same quantities as
**volume fraction (cm³/cm³)**.
:::

## The notebooks

Three visualizations were developed from published models, including ROSETTA3 from Zhang & Schaap (2017) 
to estimate saturated hydraulic conductivity in soil; Minasny & McBratney (2018), a study of empirical 
soil water holding capacity vs organic matter content in soil; and Saxton & Rawls (2006), who developed 
soil water relationships for texture and organic matter. These visualizations were developed in 
Jupyter Notebooks, available in the
[soil-health-hydraulics](https://github.com/LimnoTech/soil-health-hydraulics) GitHub 
repository and viewable here.

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### [1 · Porosity by texture & bulk density](notebooks/1_rosetta_porosity_by_texture.html)
Total porosity, **field capacity**, and **wilting point** for every USDA texture class across
bulk densities 0.8–1.9 g/cm³. Includes plant-available and drainable water in inches per foot of
soil, plus an FAO-style storage diagram. The foundation for the other two notebooks.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### [2 · Organic-matter effects](notebooks/2_organic_matter_water_holding.html)
How adding **organic matter** (0–8%, up to 15%) shifts available and drainable water, 
via empirical methods: Rosetta3 (Zhang & Schaap, 2017), Minasny & McBratney (2018), 
and Saxton & Rawls (2006), with a texture-based porosity calibration from the UNSODA 
empirical database (Nemes, et al., 2001).
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### [3 · Conductivity & infiltration](notebooks/3_rosetta_hydraulic_conductivity.html)
Saturated hydraulic conductivity, **Ksat**, unsaturated hydraulic conductivity, **K(h)** 
from the Mualem–van Genuchten curve, and **Green–Ampt infiltration** rates, in units of 
inches per hour (in/hr), with sliders to alter bulk-density.
:::

:::

## What's behind the numbers

All results come from the **Rosetta v3** pedotransfer functions, which predict van
Genuchten–Mualem water-retention and conductivity parameters from sand/silt/clay percentages
and bulk density. Texture classes use representative *median* sand/silt/clay values. See the
individual notebooks for full methodology, assumptions, and caveats, or the
[project README](https://github.com/LimnoTech/soil-health-hydraulics#readme) for an overview.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
These are **model estimates** for matrix (non-macropore) flow, intended for relative comparison
and planning. They are not a substitute for site-specific measurements.
:::
